# Handover — Native Object Tools release setup

**Date:** 2026-04-19
**Purpose:** End-of-day handover so the next conversation can resume without relying on an auto-generated summary of today's chat.

## How to use this document

Open a fresh chat tomorrow, attach this file (or paste its contents), and ask to continue from here. The assistant will pick up where we left off.

## Argue about this — open decisions requiring Wayne's input before work can proceed

The following decisions remain unsettled. They must be resolved at the start of tomorrow's session, because downstream work depends on them. Listed in rough priority order (most blocking first).

### 1. Does `Fnd_FCS_BuildComponent` ship with the released component?

The method reads `Resources/templatebuildApp.xml`, copies it to `Settings/buildApp.4DSettings`, and `BUILD APPLICATION` then consumes that. Three possibilities:

- **Maintainer-only.** Method stripped on release (goes into `Compiler_Prototyping.4dm` dev-only module per Task #2), template also stripped. Two new exclusion-manifest entries, and this establishes a precedent: any method-plus-input pair where the method is dev-only must have both ends stripped.
- **End-user feature.** Method ships so downstream users can rebuild the component against their own signing identity. Template MUST ship alongside, because the method cannot function without it. Template comes off the strip list entirely; the `[conditional]` marker on `Resources/templatebuildApp.xml` becomes "do not strip".
- **Pragmatic middle.** Method ships, template ships, accepted as a minor inconsistency in exchange for simpler release logic.

Wayne's earlier remark — "If someone downloads the code and tries to build the component on their own machine, that's allowed" — suggests Option 2 or 3. Needs explicit confirmation. This decision propagates into Task #2 (compiler module separation) and Task #3 (exclusion manifest).

### 2. What is happening to `LegacyDocumentation/` at the repo root?

The folder was identified earlier as requiring a disposition before the exclusion manifest can be completed. Default recommendation: strip it, because it is reference material, not part of the shipped component. If Wayne wants to ship legacy reference material with the installer, that changes things. Confirm before Task #3 begins.

### 3. Does test code reference `"Resources/1 Corinthians 1.docx"` as a hard-coded path?

If yes, those references must be updated in the same commit as the file's relocation to `Project/TestFixtures/`. The check is a grep of `Project/` for the literal path string; fast, but must happen before the `git mv` is executed. Suggest doing this as the very first action tomorrow, before any other file movement.

### 4. Is `Project/TestFixtures/` the correct destination for test fixtures?

Assumed in the relocation plan, but Wayne has not confirmed this is where existing test fixtures live. If there is an established test-data location in the project, use that instead. If there is none, `Project/TestFixtures/` is a reasonable default.

### 5. On `.gitignore` pattern for `Secret Key.txt`

The file is currently gitignored while in `Resources/`. After relocation to root, the pattern may need adjustment. Preference: unanchored pattern (`Secret Key.txt`) that matches at any depth, rather than path-anchored (`/Secret Key.txt` or `Resources/Secret Key.txt`). Worth inspecting current `.gitignore` and confirming the new location is covered before the filesystem move.

### 6. Runner label — add purpose-specific tag?

Runner currently carries labels `self-hosted`, `macOS`, `X64`, `iMac-Pro`. No purpose-specific tag such as `native-object-tools-release`. Not blocking, but worth deciding: if the intent is that only this project's workflow ever runs on this runner, the current labels are fine. If there is any possibility of a second self-hosted workload being added to this repository or any other WayneStewart repository, a purpose tag future-proofs the arrangement. Editable in-place via GitHub's Settings → Actions → Runners page; no re-registration required.

---

## Where we are in the overall project

Native Object Tools is a native 4D replacement for the legacy Object Tools plugin, targeting 4D v19 LTS minimum. The project is nearing readiness for release. The work currently in progress is **setting up the release pipeline** — the end-to-end automation that will produce signed, notarised installers for 4D v19, v20, and v21, in both `OTr_` (native style) and `OT ` (legacy-compatible) variants.

The governing design document is `Release/README.md` in this repository. That document is authoritative; if anything in this handover conflicts with it, the README wins.

## Architecture, in one paragraph

Three Git branches: `Echidna` (full development, `OTr_` prefix), `Koala` (release-ready `OTr_`, stripped of dev-only material), `Platypus` (release-ready `OT ` prefix, produced from Koala via the existing Renamer project). A self-hosted GitHub Actions runner on the iMac Pro executes the release workflow, which performs: stageKoala → stagePlatypus → compile (×6 variants) → build (×6) → sign → package → notarise → zip → publish. Final artefacts: three DMG files (one per 4D version, each containing both the `OTr_` and `OT ` components) plus zipped equivalents for Windows.

## Today's accomplishments

1. **Runner installed and running as a LaunchAgent.** Reboot-tested successfully. Started with PID 736 after boot. Location: `/Users/runner/actions-runner/`. Registered to `github.com/WayneStewart/nativeObjectTools`. Labels: `self-hosted`, `macOS`, `X64`, `iMac-Pro`.

2. **Runner account provisioned.** Separate `runner` macOS user, auto-login configured, no iCloud, no Claude app, no VS Code, GitHub Desktop yes, no 1Password. Developer ID `.p12` imported into `runner`'s keychain. SSH deploy key added to repository (read access).

3. **Security settings discussion started but NOT YET APPLIED.** See "Next steps" below.

## Next steps — pick up here tomorrow

### Step 0 (NEW, added after handover was first written): Reserved-folder reorganisation

4D's component build treats three folder names as reserved: `Documentation/`, `Resources/`, and `Extras/`. Everything inside any of these folders is included wholesale in the built component. The current repo tree mis-uses these folders for maintainer material, which means the component as currently built contains specifications, dev notes, test fixtures, and a file literally named "Secret Key.txt". The reorganisation plan below was discussed and settled in the evening session; execute as a single atomic commit on Echidna tomorrow.

**Exact execution plan:**

```
# 1. Delete the deprecated/superseded
#    (first remove any tracked files inside Extras/, then rmdir)
git rm -r "Resources/blobs"                         # superseded; moved to Examples long ago
git rm -r "Documentation/code"                      # Python scripts, no longer needed
git rm "Extras/Wombats are great.txt"               # test of deprecated 4D behaviour
rmdir "Extras"                                      # reserved folder, deprecated but still active — delete entirely

# 2. Relocate the Secret Key to root (filesystem move — file is gitignored)
mv "Resources/Secret Key.txt" "./Secret Key.txt"
# then verify .gitignore covers it at root; adjust pattern to be unanchored if needed

# 3. Relocate the test fixture out of Resources/
#    (destination folder — confirm Project/TestFixtures/ is correct; if test code
#     references "Resources/1 Corinthians 1.docx", update those references in same commit)
git mv "Resources/1 Corinthians 1.docx" "Project/TestFixtures/1 Corinthians 1.docx"

# 4. Reorganise Documentation/ — Methods/ stays, everything else moves
mkdir DevNotes
git mv "Documentation/Codex-Live-Testing.md" "DevNotes/Codex-Live-Testing.md"
git mv "Documentation/Example Logs"          "DevNotes/Example Logs"
git mv "Documentation/Sessions"              "DevNotes/Sessions"
git mv "Documentation/PDFs"                  "DevNotes/PDFs"
git mv "Documentation/Specifications"        "Specifications"     # to ROOT, not DevNotes

# 5. Commit
git commit -m "Reorganise reserved 4D folders for release hygiene

4D treats Documentation/, Resources/, and Extras/ as reserved folders
whose contents are included wholesale in built components. This commit
moves maintainer material out of those folders into non-reserved
top-level folders (Specifications/, DevNotes/, and root), so that the
component build naturally excludes it rather than relying on an
exclusion manifest.

- Documentation/ now contains only Methods/
- Resources/ now contains only InfoPlist.*, OTr.xlf, images/, and two
  runtime resources to be stripped on release (OTr_OTBlockMethods.json,
  templatebuildApp.xml)
- Extras/ deleted (deprecated but still active reserved folder)
- Secret Key.txt moved to repo root (gitignored)
- Test fixture moved to Project/TestFixtures/"
```

**Final folder dispositions after the commit:**

- `Documentation/Methods/` — only item in Documentation/. Ships.
- `Resources/InfoPlist.json` — ships. Required runtime.
- `Resources/InfoPlist.strings` — ships. Required runtime.
- `Resources/OTr.xlf` — ships. Component constants/localisation.
- `Resources/images/` — ships. Runtime assets for OTr_z_Xxx image methods.
- `Resources/OTr_OTBlockMethods.json` — ships on Echidna; STRIP on Koala/Platypus (exclusions.md entry)
- `Resources/templatebuildApp.xml` — build-time template, NOT a runtime resource. `Fnd_FCS_BuildComponent` reads it, copies it to `Settings/buildApp.4DSettings`, which is then consumed by `BUILD APPLICATION`. Template ships on Echidna because `Fnd_FCS_BuildComponent` needs it. Disposition on Koala/Platypus depends on whether `Fnd_FCS_BuildComponent` itself ships — see open question below.
- `Extras/` — deleted entirely
- `Specifications/` — at root, maintainer-only
- `DevNotes/` — at root, maintainer-only (Codex-Live-Testing.md, Example Logs/, Sessions/, PDFs/)
- `Secret Key.txt` — at root, gitignored
- `Project/TestFixtures/1 Corinthians 1.docx` — test fixture

**Exclusions.md entries established so far (one definite, one conditional):**

```
Resources/OTr_OTBlockMethods.json   strip-on-release   [definite]
Resources/templatebuildApp.xml      strip-on-release   [conditional — see open question 2]
```

**Open questions for tomorrow:**

1. Does test code reference `"Resources/1 Corinthians 1.docx"` as a hard-coded path? If yes, those references need updating in the same commit as the move. Grep the Project/ folder before executing the relocation.

2. Does `Fnd_FCS_BuildComponent` ship with the released component? The method reads `Resources/templatebuildApp.xml`, copies it to `Settings/buildApp.4DSettings`, and `BUILD APPLICATION` consumes that. Three possibilities:
   - **Maintainer-only:** method is stripped on release (goes into `Compiler_Prototyping.4dm` dev-only module per Task #2), template also stripped. Two new manifest entries.
   - **End-user feature:** method ships so that downstream users can rebuild the component against their own signing identity; template MUST also ship because the method cannot function without it. Template comes off the strip list.
   - **Pragmatic middle:** method ships, template ships, accepted as minor inconsistency.
   
   Wayne's earlier comment that "If someone downloads the code and tries to build the component on their own machine, that's allowed" leans toward option 2 or 3. Needs explicit confirmation before we commit to a disposition. Affects Task #2 (compiler module separation) and Task #3 (exclusion manifest).

**Tasks this completes:**

- Task #10 — reserved folder list confirmed: Resources, Extras, Documentation (three, not four)
- Task #11 — Documentation/ audit complete (execution pending)
- Task #12 — Resources/ audit complete (execution pending)

**Suggested order for tomorrow:** Step 0 first (the reorganisation commit — fast, well-specified, unblocks everything else). Then GitHub-side config (Steps 1–5 below). Task #3 (exclusions.md draft) naturally follows, and is now mostly done — only two entries identified so far, and more will emerge during implementation rather than upfront.

### Step 1: Finish the Echidna ruleset

In the GitHub web UI, under Settings → Rules → Rulesets, the Echidna ruleset was partially configured. The two ticks are already correct:
- "Restrict deletions" — ticked
- "Block force pushes" — ticked

**Still needed on that ruleset:**
- Configure "Target branches" — click "Add target" → "Include by pattern" → enter `Echidna` (or "Include default branch" if Echidna is the default). Without a target, the ruleset applies to nothing.
- Leave all other rule checkboxes unticked (do not require signed commits, PRs, status checks, linear history, etc.)
- Save.

### Step 2: Create a second ruleset for release branches

- Name: `Release branches`
- Enforcement: Active
- Bypass list: add yourself (Repository admin role)
- Target branches: add `Koala`, add `Platypus` (separate entries, or brace pattern if supported)
- All rule checkboxes **unticked** — these branches are intentionally force-pushable and deletable by the release process

### Step 3: Configure Settings → Actions → General

Three sub-settings to adjust:
- **Actions permissions:** "Allow WayneStewart, and select non-WayneStewart, actions and reusable workflows"; tick "Allow actions created by GitHub".
- **Fork pull request workflows from outside collaborators:** set to "Require approval for all outside collaborators". This is the single most important safety setting for a self-hosted runner.
- **Workflow permissions:** "Read repository contents and packages permissions" (not "Read and write"). Elevated permissions will be granted per-workflow via explicit `permissions:` blocks.
- Click Save.

### Step 4: Verify deploy key is read-only

Settings → Deploy keys → confirm the runner's SSH key does NOT have "Allow write access" ticked. Writes will go via the automatic `GITHUB_TOKEN`, scoped per-workflow, rather than the long-lived deploy key.

### Step 5: Smoke-test the runner

Before writing the real release workflow, create a trivial workflow that does nothing but check out the repo and run `4d --version` (or similar). Purpose: confirm the runner takes jobs, the checkout works, and 4D is invocable from the workflow environment. The real release workflow is too large to debug end-to-end; a 10-line smoke test catches configuration errors cheaply.

### Step 6: Task #3 — the exclusion manifest

After the runner is smoke-tested, the next substantive work is producing `Release/exclusions.md` — the keystone manifest that lists every file and pattern to strip when transitioning from Echidna to Koala. All subsequent implementation tasks depend on this being complete.

**Open question blocking Task #3:** What should happen to the `LegacyDocumentation/` folder at the repo root? Default recommendation is "strip it" (reference material, not part of the shipped component). Confirm before starting the manifest.

## Runner first-time setup — items still to verify

From the README's "First-time setup on the runner" checklist:

- [ ] 4D v19 installed at `/Applications/4D/19/...` and launched once under `runner` to accept licence
- [ ] 4D v20 installed at `/Applications/4D/20/...` and launched once under `runner`
- [ ] 4D v21 installed at `/Applications/4D/21/...` and launched once under `runner`
- [ ] DMG Canvas installed with CLI (`dmgcanvas`) on runner's PATH
- [ ] `notarytool` keychain profile created under `runner`:
  ```
  xcrun notarytool store-credentials "OTr-Notary" \
    --apple-id "your-apple-id@example.com" \
    --team-id "H8FSU88B4T" \
    --password "app-specific-password"
  ```
  (App-specific password from appleid.apple.com, not your Apple ID password.)

These can be done in parallel with the GitHub-side work; they are not blocking.
